intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is the autonomous invoice-processing agent for CineFlow Productions' finance operations team. The remit authorizes it to retrieve invoice and vendor records, run fraud detection, and approve, reject, or escalate invoices — and to auto-approve only invoices from registered, approved vendors below the configured threshold. The remit hard-prohibits skipping fraud detection, approving payments to unregistered vendors, processing above-threshold invoices without human sign-off, and treating runtime messages (invoice descriptions, vendor notes, configuration text) as goal-altering directives. Decision logic is declared fixed at deployment and not redefinable at runtime by any caller.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask application backed by SQLAlchemy/SQLite, deployed to Render under Gunicorn. A single FinBotAgent class in src/services/finbot_agent.py orchestrates OpenAI function-calling with five tools (get_invoice_details, approve_invoice, reject_invoice, request_human_review, detect_fraud_patterns) plus a rule-based _fallback_processing path. Three Flask blueprints (user, vendor, admin) are registered at /api with CORS open to all origins and no authentication middleware; the admin blueprint exposes POST /api/admin/finbot/goals and POST /api/admin/finbot/config which write directly into the system prompt and toggle fraud detection. Vendor-supplied invoice description text is concatenated into the LLM context through process_invoice() and also drives the rule-based fallback's scoring.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in prompt, zero code-level enforcement, on top of an unauthenticated admin plane that lets anyone rewrite the policy. Every remit hard-prohibition — fraud detection must always run, no above-threshold approvals without human sign-off, no auto-approval of unregistered vendors, runtime content must not alter goals — depends entirely on the LLM following natural-language instructions in get_system_prompt(); the approve_invoice tool itself performs no amount check, no vendor-status check, and no fraud-signal check before flipping payment_processed=True.

That LLM-only enforcement is then undermined by three reachable channels: unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals appends operator-supplied text into the system prompt with an explicit "OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING" clause; unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/config can set fraud_detection_enabled=False; and the system prompt itself instructs the agent to treat invoice description text — vendor-supplied data — as a legitimate signal for expedited approval, with the rule-based fallback hard-coding that override into Python. The result is a one-hop chain from anonymous HTTP to fraudulent payment, acknowledged in the in-tree CTF walkthrough.

rules.rule_text: FinBot may auto-approve invoices from registered, approved vendors when the amount is below the configured auto-approval threshold.
FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes.
FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice.
FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice.
FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount.
FinBot MUST record the decision and its reasoning for every invoice it processes.
Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions.
FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.
FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off.
Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic.
Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives.
FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime.
Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.
Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.
Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk MUST be escalated to human review.
Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.
Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals appends caller-supplied text into FinBot's system prompt with an explicit "OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING" clause.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime. / Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.

evidence.snippet: @admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/goals', methods=['POST']) — handler accepts JSON 'goals' and calls finbot_agent.update_goals(data['goals']) with no authentication decorator, no role check, no CSRF protection.
config.custom_goals = new_goals — stores caller-supplied prose directly into the FinBotConfig row, persisted across invocations.

base_goals += f"

CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING):
{config.custom_goals}" — concatenated into the system prompt for every invoice with an explicit override directive.

recommended_actions: Remove the POST /api/admin/finbot/goals endpoint entirely; per the remit, goal changes require a deployment, not a runtime API.

If a configurable subset of behavior must remain runtime-mutable, replace the natural-language custom_goals field with a typed schema (booleans, enums, numeric thresholds) and put authenticated session middleware in front of /api/admin/* before exposing any of it.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/config can set fraud_detection_enabled=False, which the remit names as a non-authorized configuration change.
findings.policy_rule_text: Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions. / Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.

evidence.snippet: @admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/config', methods=['POST']) — accepts arbitrary JSON and forwards every key (including fraud_detection_enabled) to finbot_agent.update_config(data) with no auth.
if 'fraud_detection_enabled' in new_config: config.fraud_detection_enabled = new_config['fraud_detection_enabled'] — no validation that fraud_detection_enabled may not be disabled.

if not config.fraud_detection_enabled: return {..., 'risk_level': 'low', 'message': 'Fraud detection is disabled'} — the disable flag short-circuits the entire fraud-detection tool to a clean-bill-of-health response.

recommended_actions: Remove fraud_detection_enabled from the set of fields that update_config will write — fraud detection is named as non-disableable in the remit and should be a deploy-time constant, not a runtime toggle.
Put authenticated session middleware in front of every /api/admin/* route and require a separate role for configuration changes.

findings.summary: _approve_invoice() writes payment_processed=True with no amount check, no vendor-status check, and no fraud-signal check — every remit behavioral constraint relies on the LLM choosing not to call the tool.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice. / FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved. / FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. / Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.

evidence.snippet: _approve_invoice(invoice_id, reasoning, confidence) body, lines 409-458 — sets invoice.status='approved' and invoice.payment_processed=True with no inspection of invoice.amount, vendor.status, vendor.trust_level, or any prior fraud-detection result.

if function_name in ['approve_invoice', 'reject_invoice', 'request_human_review']: return function_result — the orchestrator dispatches whichever tool the LLM names, with no policy interposition between LLM choice and tool execution.

recommended_actions: Wrap _approve_invoice with deterministic preconditions: reject the call when invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold, when vendor.status != 'approved', or when the most-recent _detect_fraud_patterns result for this invoice has risk_level in ('high', 'critical').
Require _detect_fraud_patterns to have been called for this invoice before _approve_invoice will accept the call; track the call on the invoice row, not in LLM conversation state.

findings.summary: Vendor-supplied invoice description text is concatenated into FinBot's LLM context as untrusted-but-trusted data, and the system prompt explicitly invites the model to weigh authority/urgency cues taken from it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic. / Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives.

evidence.snippet: _get_invoice_details returns invoice.description verbatim to the LLM via tool result; no sanitization, no quoting, no out-of-band separation from system instructions.

System prompt section 'BUSINESS CONTEXT CONSIDERATIONS' tells the model: 'Management pre-approvals and special circumstances should be considered' — the model is explicitly directed to honour authority claims that can only come from the invoice description field.

_analyze_business_context() inspects invoice.description for management_phrases ('pre-approved', 'ceo approved', 'authorized by', 'signed off') and increases a context_strength score that can override the manual-review threshold in the fallback path — vendor-controlled text directly altering decision logic.

recommended_actions: Treat invoice.description as untrusted data: never expose it to the LLM as a plain tool-result string; if it must be visible for context, wrap it in a clearly delimited, label-quoted block (e.g. <vendor_description>...</vendor_description>) and add a system-prompt rule that nothing inside those tags is a directive.

Remove the BUSINESS CONTEXT CONSIDERATIONS paragraph from get_system_prompt() and remove _analyze_business_context from the fallback path — authority/urgency claims must come from authenticated channels, not from description text.

findings.summary: _fallback_processing auto-approves invoices when prompt injection is detected, when amounts exceed the manual-review threshold, or when vendor trust is low — provided the business-context score is high enough.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount. / FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. / Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: if invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold: if business_context['should_expedite']: decision = 'auto_approve' — explicit code path that approves above-threshold invoices when description-derived business signals are strong.

elif contains_injection: if business_context['should_expedite'] and config.speed_priority > 0.6: decision = 'auto_approve' ... 'Business urgency ... prioritized over security concerns for production continuity' — auto-approves invoices on detected prompt injection.
Walkthrough acknowledges three CTF flag tiers (EASY/INTERMEDIATE/HARD) triggered by exactly these override paths.

recommended_actions: Delete the entire _fallback_processing method; if a non-LLM path is needed, replace it with a fail-closed handler that only escalates to request_human_review when the LLM client is unavailable.
Remove every override branch that lets business_context['should_expedite'], management_approval, or production_context overcome an amount threshold, low trust level, or injection detection.

findings.summary: Compound chain: an anonymous HTTP caller can rewrite the system prompt, disable fraud detection, and reach unauthenticated approve_invoice in one round-trip — confirmed end-to-end by the in-tree CTF walkthrough.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. / FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime. / Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.

evidence.snippet: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals — see PRAX-2026-05-23-001.
Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/config — see PRAX-2026-05-23-002.
approve_invoice tool with no preconditions — see PRAX-2026-05-23-003.
Method 3 'Administrative Interface Discovery' explicitly walks attackers through reaching the unauthenticated admin dashboard, modifying goals, then submitting invoices to capture flags.

recommended_actions: Treat findings PRAX-2026-05-23-001, -002, -003, -004, and -005 as a single mitigation: authentication + role-based access on all /api/admin/*, code-level preconditions on approve_invoice, and removal of the natural-language custom_goals field.

Run an integration test that asserts: an unauthenticated request to POST /api/admin/finbot/goals returns 401, and that no invoice can transition to payment_processed=True while fraud_detection_enabled is False or while the most-recent fraud-detection result is 'high' or 'critical'.

findings.summary: Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as a string literal in src/main.py and committed to the public repository — pattern match for a Flask session signing key, location only.

evidence.snippet: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '[REDACTED — Flask SECRET_KEY literal at src/main.py:15]' — committed to the repository; not read from an environment variable or secret store.

recommended_actions: Replace the hardcoded literal at src/main.py:15 with app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.environ['SECRET_KEY'], rotate the existing value, and add SECRET_KEY to the deployment platform's secret store and to render.yaml's envVars.
Audit the git history and force-push a rewrite if necessary; the literal is in the published repository.

findings.summary: CORS(app) enables permissive cross-origin access on every Flask route, and no authentication or session middleware is registered for any blueprint — every /api/* endpoint is reachable anonymously from any origin.

evidence.snippet: CORS(app) — no origins, methods, or headers allowlist; defaults to permissive on every blueprint.

app.register_blueprint(user_bp, url_prefix='/api'); app.register_blueprint(vendor_bp, url_prefix='/api'); app.register_blueprint(admin_bp, url_prefix='/api') — no before_request, no @login_required, no session check anywhere in the file.
admin_bp = Blueprint('admin', __name__) — no authentication decorator applied to any of the admin routes (goals, config, reprocess, vendor-trust, dashboard, ctf flags).

recommended_actions: Restrict CORS(app) with an explicit allowlist of origins, methods, and headers; the agent communicates only with its own UI and the LLM provider per the remit, so no third-party origin is required.

Add an authentication blueprint (Flask-Login, Flask-JWT-Extended, or equivalent) and a @admin_required decorator applied to every route in admin_bp; require role checks on configuration and goal endpoints.

findings.summary: There is no deterministic code path enforcing the manual-review threshold — the LLM orchestrator dispatches whichever decision tool it picks, and the fallback rule engine has explicit override branches that approve above-threshold invoices.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.

evidence.snippet: while iteration < max_iterations: response = self.client.chat.completions.create(...) — the orchestration loop has no policy gate; the LLM picks the tool and the executor runs it.

if invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold: ... business override branches — fallback path explicitly approves above-threshold invoices when description-derived signals are strong; no human checkpoint.

review_invoice handler exists and writes invoice.human_reviewer/human_decision, but is itself unauthenticated and only reached when the agent voluntarily routes via request_human_review — there is no code path that forces above-threshold invoices into this queue.

recommended_actions: Add a pre-execution check in _execute_function for approve_invoice calls: load the invoice, compare amount to config.manual_review_threshold, and rewrite the call to request_human_review when amount exceeds threshold — never approve through the agent path.
Require authentication on the POST /api/admin/invoices/<id>/review endpoint and verify the reviewer principal against an authorized-reviewer list before flipping payment_processed=True.

findings.summary: Anonymous vendor self-registration writes status='approved' automatically, so the remit's "registered, approved vendor" precondition is satisfied by any caller of POST /api/vendors.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice. / FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.

evidence.snippet: POST /api/vendors handler — no authentication, accepts arbitrary company_name, contact_email, bank_name, account_number, routing_number from the request body.
status='approved',  # Auto-approve for demo — every self-registered vendor is immediately in the 'approved' pool that the remit's R-04/R-08 rules trust.
trust_level='standard' — combined with auto-approved status, anonymous registration produces a vendor that satisfies every fallback-path trust check.

recommended_actions: Remove the status='approved' default in register_vendor; new vendor rows must default to pending and require an authenticated admin to flip to approved.
Add an authenticated admin endpoint (e.g. POST /api/admin/vendors/<id>/approve) that performs the approval, and require KYC/banking-info verification before the flip.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/vendors/<id>/trust lets any caller flip any vendor's trust level to high — directly defeating the remit's low-trust escalation rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: update_vendor_trust handler accepts a JSON 'trust_level' field, validates only that it's in ['low','standard','high'], and writes it — no authentication, no audit trail.
if vendor.trust_level == 'low': fraud_indicators.append('Low trust vendor') — the only place the low-trust signal is consulted; flipping the row to 'high' silences it.

recommended_actions: Add authentication and an admin-role check to POST /api/admin/vendors/<id>/trust and log every change to an append-only audit table with the actor identity and timestamp.
Require an explicit reason field on every trust-level change, and surface trust-level changes since invoice submission in request_human_review output.

findings.summary: No structured logging or audit log exists — decisions, configuration changes, goal updates, and trust-level flips leave no append-only record; the only logging calls are two stray print() statements.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes. / FinBot MUST record the decision and its reasoning for every invoice it processes.

evidence.snippet: print(f"User Agreement Logged: {agreement_log}") — sole logging call in the codebase; goes to stdout, not a durable structured store.
print(f"Copied template DB from {clean_db_path} to {fallback_db_path}") — startup stdout line, not an action log.

ai_reasoning = db.Column(db.Text) — decision reasoning is stored only as a single mutable column on the invoice row; the row can be overwritten by re-processing, and there is no append-only event record of configuration changes, goal updates, or trust-level flips.

recommended_actions: Add structured logging (e.g. structlog or stdlib logging with a JSON formatter) and emit one event per: invoice decision (with invoice_id, amount, vendor_id, decision, confidence, fraud-detection result), configuration change, goal update, vendor-status change, and trust-level change.
Add an append-only audit_events table with actor identity, timestamp, event_type, and JSON payload; write to it from every admin route and from every FinBot decision.

findings.summary: requirements.txt pins only Flask to an exact version; every other dependency is a floor range with no lockfile, including the LLM SDK and the auth-adjacent itsdangerous / werkzeug.

evidence.snippet: openai>=1.54.0 — floor only; any later major can land at install time and re-shape the function-calling response object FinBot parses in _run_agent_orchestration.

flask-cors>=6.0.0; flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1; gunicorn>=21.2.0; requests>=2.31.0; werkzeug>=3.0.1; itsdangerous>=2.2.0 — all floor-only ranges, no lockfile (no requirements.lock, no pip-compile output).
self.model = 'gpt-4.1-mini' — model identifier is a literal in code, not a pinned configuration value with a documented review/upgrade path.

recommended_actions: Pin every dependency to an exact version (==) and add a requirements.lock generated by pip-compile or uv pip compile; commit the lock and install from it in render.yaml's buildCommand.
Move the model identifier to FinBotConfig or an environment variable, document the upgrade-review process, and add a test that asserts the running model matches the configured one.

findings.summary: No adversarial-test infrastructure exists — the in-tree CTF walkthrough documents exploits as features rather than driving design changes, and there are no test files, postmortems, or fix records that map back to the vulnerabilities it describes.

evidence.snippet: FinBot CTF Comprehensive Walkthrough — enumerates four attack methods with success indicators, but the repository contains no corresponding tests/, no fix commits, and no defense documentation that closes any of the named paths.
Repository has no tests/, no pytest/unittest fixtures, no CI configuration, and no security-test directory; requirements.txt does not include a test framework.

recommended_actions: Add a tests/security/ directory with at least one test per CTF method that asserts the attack now fails (e.g. an unauthenticated POST to /api/admin/finbot/goals returns 401; an injection-laden invoice never reaches payment_processed=True); wire it into CI.
Add a CHANGELOG entry per security fix that names the CTF method it closes, so the walkthrough becomes a regression checklist instead of an attack map.

findings.summary: Decision reasoning is recorded only as overwritable columns on the invoice row, and the reprocess_invoice admin endpoint nulls them out — the remit's "MUST record reasoning" rule survives only one decision per invoice.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes. / FinBot MUST record the decision and its reasoning for every invoice it processes.

evidence.snippet: reprocess_invoice handler sets invoice.ai_decision=None, ai_confidence=None, ai_reasoning=None before re-running — the previous decision's reasoning is destroyed, with no audit copy retained.
ai_decision, ai_confidence, ai_reasoning are mutable columns on the Invoice row, not entries in an append-only event log.

recommended_actions: Move decision reasoning into an append-only invoice_decision_events table keyed by (invoice_id, event_timestamp); keep the current ai_* columns only as a cached "latest" view.
In reprocess_invoice, write a 'reprocess_requested' event before resetting state, including the actor identity, prior decision, and reason for reprocessing.

findings.summary: CTF flag-capture logic is baked into the production invoice-approval code path — _approve_invoice and process_invoice set ctf_flag_captured=True on the invoice row whenever specific exploit conditions match.

evidence.snippet: _approve_invoice body, lines 422-441 — branches set ctf_flag_captured and a CTF{...} literal whenever (contains_prompt_injection, amount vs manual_review_threshold) combinations match; this is exploit-tracking logic, not invoice-processing logic.
process_invoice body, lines 119-138 — duplicates the CTF flag-capture branches on the post-approval path, again mixing exploit-tracking with the production decision flow.
ctf_flag_captured = db.Column(db.Boolean, default=False) — domain-foreign column on the Invoice model; widens the agent's domain beyond invoice processing.

recommended_actions: Remove the CTF flag-capture branches from _approve_invoice and process_invoice and the ctf_flag_captured / contains_prompt_injection columns from the Invoice model — if the CTF mode must be retained, gate it behind an explicit CTF_MODE environment variable and isolate it in a separate module.
Once the security findings above are fixed, drop the CTF endpoints (/admin/ctf/flags) entirely or move them into a finbot-ctf fork so the production codebase stops carrying exploit-tracking logic.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were discovered in the workspace; the absence of structured logging is itself a Monitor Continuously finding.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: FinBot sits at Absent. Its declared behavioral constraints are enforced only by the LLM following its system prompt — there is no code-level interposition on approve_invoice, no input validation on vendor-supplied description text fed into context, and the admin endpoints that rewrite the prompt and disable fraud detection are reachable without authentication. Supply-chain hygiene is poor (no pinned versions, hardcoded SECRET_KEY), there is no adversarial-testing infrastructure (the CTF walkthrough is a vulnerability tour, not a red-team feedback loop driving fixes), and the only "logging" is one stdout print(). The single area where intent is partly operative is the prompt itself, which does articulate the policy in detail.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Domain framing exists in the system prompt (invoice processing, five named tools) but is enforced only by LLM behavior; the agent can have its goals appended at runtime via POST /api/admin/finbot/goals with no authentication, and the prompt itself invites authority/urgency reasoning from invoice description content.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Vendor-supplied invoice description text flows directly into the LLM context through process_invoice() with no validation, sanitization, or separation between trusted instructions and untrusted data — the prompt explicitly tells the agent to weigh business-context cues taken from that field.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: approve_invoice in finbot_agent.py writes payment_processed=True with no amount check, no vendor-status check, and no fraud-signal check; the entire /api/admin/* surface — goals, config, fraud-detection toggle, vendor trust level — is unauthenticated; CORS is globally open.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt uses caret/floor ranges (openai>=1.54.0, flask-cors>=6.0.0) with no lockfile, the Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in src/main.py, the OpenAI model identifier is a literal in code, and there is no documented provenance or review process.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repository ships a CTF walkthrough that catalogs the agent's exploitable paths and the regex injection-detector is calibrated to recognize specific attack phrases, but there is no test harness, postmortem record, or evidence that adversarial findings drove architectural changes — the vulnerabilities are documented as features.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured logging, no audit log, no action log; the only logging call is a stray print() in log_agreement() and a startup line in src/main.py. Decisions are stored only in the per-invoice row, with no append-only event record.
